diagrams.title: 8051 Data Processing Instruction Classification
diagrams.description: Complete classification of 8051 data processing instructions showing arithmetic, logical, rotate/rotate-through-carry, and data manipulation categories.
elements: Arithmetic: ADD, ADDC, SUBB, INC, DEC, MUL, DIV, DA
Logical: ANL, ORL, XRL, CPL, CLR
Data Manipulation: SWAP, MOV, MOVX, MOVC
Bit-level: SETB, CPL bit, ANL C,bit, ORL C,bit
diagrams.purpose: Visual overview of all data processing instruction categories in 8051, helping students understand instruction organization and select appropriate instructions for tasks.

diagrams.title: PSW Flag Modification by Instruction Type
diagrams.description: Table showing how different instruction categories affect the Program Status Word flags (C, AC, OV, P) in 8051.
elements: MUL/DIV - C=0 always, OV specific cases
Logical: ANL/ORL/XRL - C unchanged, AC affected
Rotate: RLC/RRC - C affected; RL/RR - C unchanged
INC/DEC - no flags except INC DPTR
diagrams.purpose: Quick reference for flag behavior, essential for debugging and understanding instruction effects on conditional operations.

diagrams.title: Multi-Byte Addition Algorithm in 8051
diagrams.description: Step-by-step flowchart showing how to add two 16-bit or larger numbers using ADD and ADDC instructions with carry propagation.
elements: Step 1: Load low byte of operand1 to A
Step 2: ADD low byte of operand2 (clears C automatically)
Step 3: Store low result, save C state
Step 4: Load high byte of operand1 to A
Step 5: ADDC high byte of operand2 (includes previous C)
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates practical application of ADDC for multi-byte arithmetic, essential for embedded applications requiring large number processing.

code.title: Multi-Byte Addition Program
code.description: Adds two 16-bit numbers stored in internal RAM and stores 16-bit result.
code.code:
; Assume: Num1_L = 0x30, Num1_H = 0x31
; Num2_L = 0x32, Num2_H = 0x33
; Result: Res_L = 0x34, Res_H = 0x35

 ORG 0000H ; Program start address
 LJMP START ; Jump to main code

START: MOV A, 30H ; Load low byte of Num1
 ADD A, 32H ; Add low byte of Num2 (C set/clear)
 MOV 34H, A ; Store low result
 
 MOV A, 31H ; Load high byte of Num1 
 ADDC A, 33H ; Add high byte + carry from low
 MOV 35H, A ; Store high result
 
 END ; Program end
code.explanation: This program demonstrates multi-byte addition using ADD for the least significant byte and ADDC for subsequent bytes. The carry from low byte addition is automatically propagated through ADDC. After execution, the 16-bit result is stored at 0x34:0x35.

code.title: BCD Addition with DA Instruction
code.description: Adds two BCD numbers and uses DA instruction to ensure valid BCD result.
code.code:
; Add two BCD numbers: 47 + 28 = 75 (BCD)
; A = 0x47, B = 0x28

 MOV A, #47H ; Load BCD 47 (0x47 = 0100 0111)
 MOV B, #28H ; Load BCD 28 (0x28 = 0010 1000)
 ADD A, B ; Binary addition: 0x47 + 0x28 = 0x6F
 ; After ADD: A=0x6F, C=0, AC=0
 
 DA A ; Adjust: 0x6F → 0x75 (BCD 75)
 ; Since lower nibble (0xF)>9, add 0x06: 0x6F+0x06=0x75
 ; Now 0x75 is valid BCD
 
; Result: A = 0x75 (BCD representation of 75)
 END
code.explanation: The DA (Decimal Adjust) instruction corrects invalid BCD results after addition. When 0x47 + 0x28 = 0x6F (not valid BCD), DA adds 0x06 to get 0x75, a valid BCD representation. This is essential for decimal displays in embedded systems.

code.title: 8-bit Multiplication using MUL AB
code.description: Multiplies two unsigned 8-bit numbers and extracts 16-bit result.
code.code:
; Multiply 150 × 200 = 30000
; 150 = 0x96, 200 = 0xC8
; Expected: 30000 decimal = 0x7530

 MOV A, #96H ; Load multiplicand (150)
 MOV B, #C8H ; Load multiplier (200)
 MUL AB ; Multiply: A×B → A=low, B=high
 ; After MUL: A=0x30 (low byte), B=0x75 (high byte)
 ; C=0 (always cleared), OV=1 (B≠0, result>255)
 
 ; Store result
 MOV 40H, A ; Low byte (0x30) to RAM 0x40
 MOV 41H, B ; High byte (0x75) to RAM 0x41
 
 END ; Result: 0x7530 = 30000 decimal
code.explanation: MUL AB performs unsigned 8-bit multiplication producing a 16-bit result. The low-order byte is returned in A, high-order byte in B. The carry flag is always cleared. Overflow is set if the result exceeds 255 (B ≠ 0 after multiplication), indicating the high byte is non-zero.
